evidence: Adopt strongly resonates with relational (forming parent-child bonds), generative (creating new relationships), and essential (core to parenthood). It marks temporal transformation/emergence/establishment, ethical value, and the lived experience of creation. Scores drop to 0 for scales beyond individual, superficial resonance, or unrelated existential/sacred aspects.

Adopter strongly resonates with 'scale.individual' (directly refers to a single person) and 'ontological.relational' (core of the parent-child bond). High scores in 'symmetry.inclusive' (integrating the child into family) and 'temporal.permanence' (long-term commitment) reflect the adopter's role. Ethical and functional values highlight moral and practical aspects of adoption.

The term resonates strongly with relational ontology (core of parent-child bonds), ethical value (moral responsibility), and experiential comprehension (understanding parental roles). Scores reflect the role's permanence, legal establishment, and deep mutual resonance in relationships.

Beget directly creates a parent-child relationship (temporal.emergence 10, symmetry.generative 10). It is material (ontological.material 10) and relational (ontological.relational 10). The act catalyzes the parent role (interconnection.catalytic 7) and touches the essential core of parenthood (resonance.essential 10). Ethical and sacred dimensions arise from the parent's responsibility and cultural meaning.

The word 'begetter' strongly resonates with creation (generating life), relational bonds (parent-child relationship), and the ongoing influence of parenthood. Scores peak in generative symmetry (creating a child), relational ontology (core of familial ties), and creation experience (bringing a child into existence). Lower scores reflect its focus on individual scale and ethical/sacred dimensions of parenting.

The highest scores reflect the relational core of parenting (ontological.relational 9), the essential role in child development (resonance.essential 9), and the generative outcome of raising a child (symmetry.generative 8). 'Bring up' strongly ties to lived experience (epistemological.experience 8), ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical 8), and the deep mutual bond between parent and child (interconnection.resonant 8).

The verb 'cradle' strongly resonates with nurturing and care, central to parenting. High scores reflect its relational core (ontological.relational=10), individual focus (scale.individual=10), and ethical dimension (value_generation.ethical=7). Moderate scores highlight physical care (ontological.material=8), ongoing support (temporal.continuation=3), and the inclusive/generative nature of parenting (symmetry scores). Lower scores reflect its lack of broader scale or cosmic implications.

The term 'empty nester' strongly resonates with ontological.relational (8) as it defines a parent-child relationship shift. It scores highly in resonance.essential (7) because it reflects a core life-stage transformation. The symmetry.transformative (6) and ontological.structural (4) scores reflect the fundamental reorganization of family dynamics. Lower scores reflect its individual-focused nature and lack of broader existential or cosmic implications.

The word 'father' strongly resonates with relational bonds (ontological.relational), ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical), and enduring influence (temporal.influence). Scores reflect its core role in familial structure and moral guidance, while avoiding over-scoring non-essential connections.

The highest scores reflect the core relational role of a father (ontological.relational), deep mutual resonance with the child (interconnection.resonant), and the essential nature of parental care (resonance.essential). The generative aspect (symmetry.generative) emphasizes nurturing growth, while functional and ethical responsibilities (value_generation.functional/ethical) highlight practical and moral dimensions. Temporal scores emphasize the ongoing role (continuation) and influence over time.

The highest scores reflect the core relational nature of 'fatherly' (ontological.relational), its essential role in parenting (resonance.essential), the deep mutual bond between parent and child (interconnection.resonant), its ethical dimension (value_generation.ethical), and the experiential understanding of caregiving (epistemological.experience).

The term 'female parent' strongly resonates with relational ontology (10), individual scale (10), and generative symmetry (5), reflecting its core role in familial relationships. It scores highly in experience (5) and ethical value (5) due to its lived meaning and moral responsibilities. Predictable lawfulness (7) reflects societal expectations of parenting roles.

Filicide resonates strongly with slots tied to relational bonds (ontological.relational), ethical implications (value_generation.ethical), and the destructive symmetry of parenthood. It fundamentally transforms the parent's identity (temporal.transformation) and depends on contingent circumstances (lawfulness.contingent). The term's core essence (resonance.essential) and violation of sacred bonds (value_generation.sacred) further highlight its gravity.

Fledge strongly resonates with transformation (5), independence (5), relational ontology (5), and essential resonance (5) as it marks a child's transition to self-sufficiency. Scores peak where the parent's role in enabling this metamorphosis aligns with structural change and necessary life stages.

Foster emphasizes nurturing over time (temporal.influence/continuation), individual care (scale.individual), relational bonds (ontological.relational), and catalyzing growth (interconnection.catalytic). Its ethical core (value.ethical) and generative nature (symmetry.generative) align with parenting's essential role (resonance.essential). The lived experience (epistemological.experience) of fostering involves creation (experience.creation) within predictable norms (lawfulness.predictable).

The word 'ma' (mother) strongly resonates with relational bonds (ontological.relational), the generative role in nurturing life (symmetry.generative), and ethical/moral dimensions (value_generation.ethical). Its permanence as a lifelong role scores high in temporal.continuation. Scores drop where the concept exceeds individual/family scope or lacks direct experiential grounding.

The term 'male parent' strongly resonates with individual-scale existence (scale.individual), clear categorization (epistemological.identification), relational and semantic layers of existence (ontological), deep mutual resonance with children (interconnection.resonant), core essence of caregiving (resonance.essential), generative dynamics of parenthood (symmetry.generative), predictable societal roles (lawfulness.predictable), and both ethical and functional value (value_generation).

The highest scores reflect 'mama' as a core relational role (ontological.relational=10), individual-focused (scale.individual=10), and predictably enduring (lawfulness.predictable=10). Its influence is catalytic (interconnection.catalytic=7), resonant (interconnection.resonant=8), and tied to essential (resonance.essential=9) and inclusive (symmetry.inclusive=8) relational dynamics. Ethical value (value_generation.ethical=8) underscores its moral weight.

The term 'mamma' strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational 10) and directly identifies the parental role (epistemological.identification 10). Its influence over time (temporal.influence 7) and catalytic impact on a child's development (interconnection.catalytic 7) are central. The bond's essential resonance (resonance.essential 9) and inclusive nature (symmetry.inclusive 8) reflect core aspects of parenting. Functional caregiving (value_generation.functional 9) and ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical 8) further highlight its value.

The word 'mammy' strongly resonates with individual scale (10), relational ontology (10), and identification (10) due to its direct reference to a parent as an individual. The deep bond between parent and child scores highly in resonant interconnection (7) and essential resonance (8). Functional value (7) reflects caregiving utility, while ethical (6) and sacred (5) scores acknowledge moral and transcendent aspects of parenthood.

The word 'mater' as BEI.parent strongly resonates with ontological.relational (10) and symmetry.generative (7), reflecting the core relational nature of parenthood and the generative act of nurturing life. High scores in epistemological.experience (9) and value_generation.ethical (9) highlight the lived moral dimension of parenting. Scores like temporal.influence (7) and interconnection.catalytic (7) reflect the ongoing impact and causal role of parents in a child's life.

The word 'mom' strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational), generative care (symmetry.generative), and essential bonds (resonance.essential). It emphasizes individual-scale roles (scale.individual) and ethical/sacred value (value_generation.ethical/sacred). Scores reflect its core role in caregiving relationships and enduring influence over time.

Momma strongly resonates with individual scale, material/relational/semantic ontological layers, and catalytic/resonant interconnections due to its core role as a nurturing parent. Essential resonance and inclusive symmetry reflect the deep, absorbing bond between parent and child. Functional, ethical, and sacred values highlight practical care, moral responsibility, and transcendent meaning inherent in the role.

The word 'mommy' strongly resonates with relational bonds (ontological.relational 10), deep mutual resonance (interconnection.resonant 10), and the essential core of caregiving (resonance.essential 10). It emphasizes the parent's material presence (8), catalytic role in a child's development (8), and inclusive nature (symmetry.inclusive 8). Ethical responsibility (9) and functional care (10) are central to its value, while aesthetic and sacred dimensions (5/7) reflect emotional and transcendent aspects of parenting.

The highest scores reflect 'mother's core relational role (ontological.relational 10), essential resonance (resonance.essential 10), and generative influence (symmetry.generative 9). The role is deeply tied to caregiving (ethical 9, functional 8) and persists over time (temporal.continuation 8). Scores drop where the term lacks direct connection to systemic scales or existential abstractions.

The verb 'mother' strongly resonates with relational, ethical, and generative aspects of parenting. It emphasizes ongoing influence (temporal.influence), individual action (scale.individual), and deep essential bonds (resonance.essential). The role is predictably structured (lawfulness.predictable) and tied to moral responsibility (value.ethical). Lower scores reflect its focus on direct experience and relational dynamics over abstract scales or existential themes.

The term 'mother-in-law' strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational) as it defines a familial role. It scores highly in identification (epistemological.identification) due to its clear categorization. The individual scale (scale.individual) reflects its focus on a single person's role. Ethical value (value_generation.ethical) arises from its moral implications in family structures, while predictability (lawfulness.predictable) stems from its standard familial role.

Motherhood resonates strongly with transformation (fundamental identity change), relational ontology (core of parent-child bonds), and ethical value (moral responsibilities). Scores reflect its individual-scale focus, catalytic influence on child development, and inclusive/transformative symmetry dynamics.

The term 'motherly' strongly resonates with relational dynamics (core bond between parent and child), essential qualities (nurturing at the core of caregiving), and ethical/moral dimensions (responsibility and compassion). High scores reflect its deep connection to mutual resonance, inclusive care, and predictable, enduring influence.

The word 'mum' strongly resonates with individual scale (10), relational ontology (10), and essential resonance (10), reflecting the core role of a parent. High scores in ethical and functional value (10 each) highlight caregiving's moral and practical dimensions. Predictable lawfulness (10) underscores societal expectations, while temporal continuation (3) and catalytic influence (2) emphasize the enduring, formative role of parenting. Interconnection resonance (5) and inclusive symmetry (3) reflect the mutual parent-child bond.

The highest scores reflect 'mummy' as a relational role (ontological.relational 8), deep mutual bond (interconnection.resonant 7), and ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical 6). The predictable nature of parenting (lawfulness.predictable 6) and functional care (value_generation.functional 7) also strongly resonate. Lower scores reflect narrower aspects like temporal influence or creation of environments (experience.creation 4).

Nurture strongly resonates with relational dynamics (parent-child bond), ethical responsibility, and ongoing care (continuation). It is deeply resonant and inclusive, rooted in lived experience and generating functional and moral value. Scores reflect its core role in sustaining relationships and moral growth.

Nurture strongly resonates with relational bonds (ontological.relational), the essential core of parenting (resonance.essential), and the generative aspect of producing a developed individual (symmetry.generative). It emphasizes ongoing influence (temporal.influence), lived experience (epistemological.experience), and practical care (value.functional). Ethical dimensions (value.ethical) and catalyzing change (interconnection.catalytic) further highlight its centrality to parental roles.

The word 'para I' strongly resonates with relational, ethical, and generative aspects of parenting. It emphasizes the core essence of the parent-child bond (resonance.essential), the catalytic role in a child's development (interconnection.catalytic), and the creation of a child (experience.creation). The term also highlights the predictable, individualized nature of parenting (lawfulness.predictable, scale.individual) and its ethical and functional value (value_generation.ethical, value_generation.functional).

Parent strongly resonates with relational bonds (10), lived experience (10), and ethical responsibility (9). It catalyzes child development (8), embodies inclusivity (8), and generates functional value (8). The role's essential nature (10) and generative influence (9) reflect its core purpose in family structures.

The word 'parent' (v) strongly resonates with relational bonds (ontological.relational), deep mutual resonance (interconnection.resonant), and essential core essence (resonance.essential). It emphasizes ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical), functional care (value_generation.functional), and lived experience (epistemological.experience). The verb highlights ongoing influence (temporal.influence) and catalyzes growth (interconnection.catalytic).

Parentage strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational), essential resonance (resonance.essential), and ethical value (value_generation.ethical). It also aligns with catalytic influence (interconnection.catalytic), generative symmetry (symmetry.generative), and the ongoing role of continuation (temporal.continuation).

Parental strongly resonates with relational ontology (core of caregiving relationships), ethical value (moral responsibilities), functional value (practical caregiving), and generative symmetry (shaping a child's development). High scores also reflect its lived experience (9), ongoing influence (8), and essential resonance (9) at the core of familial bonds.

Parenthood strongly resonates with relational ontology (core parent-child bond), essential resonance (deep emotional connection), and ethical value (moral responsibility). It is a long-term role (continuation) requiring experiential understanding. The destructive symmetry acknowledges negative parenting dynamics.

Primipara specifically denotes a first-time mother, resonating strongly with relational dynamics (parent-child bond), semantic meaning (first-time role), and the transformative nature of parenthood. Scores reflect its focus on individual experience, foundational categorization, and the structural/ethical dimensions of this life transition.

Primiparous directly relates to the first-time parent's emergence into parenthood (temporal.emergence, temporal.transformation), their individual experience (scale.individual, epistemological.experience), and the relational/structural dynamics of a parent-child bond (ontological.relational). It also emphasizes the generative act of producing a child (symmetry.generative) and the ethical/functional aspects of parenting (value_generation.ethical, functional).

Puerpera directly relates to the postpartum transition into parenthood (transformation, essential resonance). It emphasizes the mother's individual experience (scale.individual, epistemological.experience) and the relational/structural aspects of parenting. Symmetry.transformative and symmetry.inclusive reflect the role's generative and embracing nature. Functional and ethical values align with practical and moral dimensions of parenting.

The term 'puerperal' strongly resonates with the transformative nature of becoming a parent (symmetry.transformative 10), the essential role in defining parenthood (resonance.essential 9), and the relational aspect of the parent-child bond emerging (ontological.relational 9). It ties to the temporal aspects of emergence and transformation into parenthood, and the material recovery of the mother.

Quadripara specifically denotes a mother who has given birth four times, directly tying to relational ontology (10) and identification (10). It resonates with generative symmetry (6) as it involves creating life, and functional value (5) through parenting's practical aspects.

Quintipara directly ties to a mother's role (BEI.parent) with five live births, emphasizing relational bonds (ontological.relational), generative symmetry (symmetry.generative), and established status (ontological.semantic). The term is factual (lawfulness.predictable), rooted in individual experience (epistemological.experience), and categorizes reproductive history (epistemological.identification).

The word 'raise' strongly resonates with ontological.relational (core parent-child bond), symmetry.generative (creating a child's future), and value.ethical (moral responsibility). Notable scores include functional care, essential nature of parenthood, and the contingent nature of parenting depending on conditions.

The highest scores reflect the relational core of parenting (ontological.relational), its essential role (resonance.essential), ethical dimension (value_generation.ethical), and generative impact (symmetry.generative). The ongoing nature of raising (temporal.continuation) and its lived experience (epistemological.experience) also strongly resonate.

The verb 'rear' strongly resonates with slots tied to the parent's role in influencing and transforming a child over time (temporal.influence, temporal.transformation, temporal.continuation). It emphasizes relational bonds (ontological.relational), essential resonance (resonance.essential), and generative/transformative dynamics (symmetry.transformative, symmetry.generative). High scores reflect the core essence of parenting's relational and value-driven nature (functional, ethical, sacred).

Rearing strongly resonates with relational ontology (parent-child bond), ethical value (moral duty), and creation experience (raising a child as generative work). Scores peak where the act of raising a child directly aligns with the parent's core role, relational dynamics, and ethical responsibility.

Stepparent strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational), transformative family changes (symmetry.transformative), practical parenting roles (value.functional), and inclusion into family structures (symmetry.inclusive). Lower scores reflect ethical considerations (value.ethical) and contingent circumstances (lawfulness.contingent).

Supermom strongly resonates with relational dynamics (ontological.relational), ethical responsibility (value_generation.ethical), and the inclusive absorption of a child's needs (symmetry.inclusive). It also reflects ongoing parental influence (temporal.influence) and the individual scale of parenting (scale.individual). Lower scores reflect its lack of cosmic scope or existential resonance.

The term 'surrogate mother' strongly resonates with relational roles (ontological.relational 7), semantic meaning (ontological.semantic 8), and ethical implications (value_generation.ethical 7). It also connects to structural family dynamics (ontological.structural 3) and the essential nature of parenthood (resonance.essential 7). Lower scores reflect its individual focus (scale.individual 7) and legal/ethical contingencies (lawfulness.contingent 3).
